quotes.quote: To ensure the proposals are both grounded and new, this process is tightly integrated with a literature review module that queries external academic databases to identify knowledge gaps and avoid rediscovering existing work. The system uses structured prompts to guide idea generation and reflection rounds to refine proposals based on the literature search results (see Supplementary Information section A.2.6 for prompts).
quotes.rationale: Describes the literature review module identifying gaps and checking novelty to refine proposals.

quotes.quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.
quotes.rationale: Describes the evaluator assessing leaf nodes to dictate progression to the next stage.

quotes.quote: During experimentation, the plots generated are fed to a VLM, which is prompted to act as a scientist and critique them. For example, it might flag nonsensical axes or issues in the quality of generated examples or suggest clearer ways to present the data. This feedback is used to generate new experimental nodes in the tree search aimed at addressing the identified issues.
quotes.rationale: Describes the VLM critiquing generated plots to drive candidate revision.

quotes.quote: When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy guided by GPT-4o, which evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics and the quality of the plots generated. The selected nodes are expanded by creating a new child node.
quotes.rationale: Describes the use of GPT-4o to evaluate and rank nodes for further expansion.

quotes.quote: The agent processes the PDF of a manuscript to produce a structured review, including numerical scores for soundness, presentation and contribution, along with a list of strengths and weaknesses and a preliminary accept or reject decision (Supplementary Information section A.3).
quotes.rationale: Describes the terminal evaluation mechanism providing a final structured review.

quotes.quote: We benchmarked The Automated Reviewer against human decisions using ICLR data from the publicly available OpenReview dataset33. The Automated Reviewer achieved a comparable balanced accuracy with humans (69% versus 66%; see Supplementary Information section A.3.2 for details) and a higher F1 score compared with inter-human group agreement (0.62 versus 0.49) in the NeurIPS 2021 consistency experiment34, for which roughly 10% of submissions were randomly selected and sent to two independent review committees, thus providing a real-world benchmark of inter-reviewer consistency (Table 1).
quotes.rationale: Provides the agreement study evidence demonstrating the reliability of the terminal Automated Reviewer.

quotes.quote: These three submissions were included among the 43 papers reviewed for the workshop. Reviewers were informed that some of the submissions were AI-generated but not which ones, ensuring a blind process.
quotes.rationale: Establishes independent human evaluation of the system's generated artifacts.

diagnostics.consequence: This creates critical ambiguity regarding whether the reported outcomes depend fundamentally on human search-control decisions (filtering) rather than the system's unaided capabilities.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: The overall process was then run to generate ideas, experiments and papers. We manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage (Supplementary Information section A.4). Had this filtering not occurred, the papers under analysis would still have been produced in their final form, just along with other papers and, thus, at a greater total cost. This process resulted in three complete manuscripts being selected for submission.
The entire scientific workflow for each paper, from ideation and coding to manuscript writing, was performed without any human modification.

diagnostics.statement: The text claims the scientific workflow was performed without human modification, yet admits that humans manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage, obscuring the true extent of the system's autonomy.

diagnostics.consequence: The robustness and unbiased nature of the system's internal tree search mechanisms remain unverified, meaning systemic failures or biases during candidate generation and pruning cannot be ruled out.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.

We benchmarked The Automated Reviewer against human decisions using ICLR data from the publicly available OpenReview dataset33. The Automated Reviewer achieved a comparable balanced accuracy with humans (69% versus 66%; see Supplementary Information section A.3.2 for details) and a higher F1 score compared with inter-human group agreement (0.62 versus 0.49) in the NeurIPS 2021 consistency experiment34, for which roughly 10% of submissions were randomly selected and sent to two independent review committees, thus providing a real-world benchmark of inter-reviewer consistency (Table 1).

diagnostics.statement: While rigorous reliability evidence (an agreement study) is provided for the terminal Automated Reviewer, no reliability, calibration, or bias studies are reported for the internal evaluators that direct the search, such as the stage transition evaluator, VLM critic, or the GPT-4o node ranker.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: The overall process was then run to generate ideas, experiments and papers. We manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage (Supplementary Information section A.4). Had this filtering not occurred, the papers under analysis would still have been produced in their final form, just along with other papers and, thus, at a greater total cost. This process resulted in three complete manuscripts being selected for submission.
The entire scientific workflow for each paper, from ideation and coding to manuscript writing, was performed without any human modification.

recommendations.proposed_action: Clarify the precise stages where human filtering was applied and report end-to-end performance metrics both with and without this manual intervention.

success_check.evidence_needed: empirical results of fully autonomous end-to-end runs without manual stage filtering

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.

We benchmarked The Automated Reviewer against human decisions using ICLR data from the publicly available OpenReview dataset33. The Automated Reviewer achieved a comparable balanced accuracy with humans (69% versus 66%; see Supplementary Information section A.3.2 for details) and a higher F1 score compared with inter-human group agreement (0.62 versus 0.49) in the NeurIPS 2021 consistency experiment34, for which roughly 10% of submissions were randomly selected and sent to two independent review committees, thus providing a real-world benchmark of inter-reviewer consistency (Table 1).

recommendations.proposed_action: Conduct and report reliability and discrimination studies for internal evaluators (e.g., stage transition and node expansion) to validate their effectiveness against human judgments.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: measured against golden dataset or human baseline
success_check.evidence_needed: agreement or discrimination metrics for intermediate evaluation steps guiding the tree search
